planting.note: Direct sow in blocks of at least 4 rows for adequate wind pollination; in zone 9 plant February–April for spring and August for fall. Golden Bantam germinates reliably in cool soil (55°F) better than modern hybrids and performs well in zone 9's spring and fall shoulder seasons.

directions: Direct sow 1–1.5 inches deep in blocks of at least 4 rows to ensure wind pollination — single-row plantings yield poorly. Golden Bantam, introduced in 1902, revolutionized sweet corn by introducing yellow kernels at a time when white was standard; it remains one of the finest-flavored heirloom sweet corns available. The open-pollinated ears are 6–7 inches long with 8 rows of deep, rich, yellow kernels that convert quickly from sugar to starch after harvest — cook or eat within hours of picking for maximum sweetness. Germination is reliable in cooler soil than modern supersweet hybrids, and the variety produces better under stress than most modern types. Save seed easily by allowing selected ears to dry fully on the stalk.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Golden Bantam Sweet Corn
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Golden Bantam
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Open-Pollinated Corn

planting.note: Soak seeds overnight before planting to speed germination; direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 65°F. In zone 9 plant March–July; Annie Oakley is earlier than most okra varieties and begins producing within 52 days of sowing, valuable for a longer harvest window.

directions: Direct sow after soil warms to 65°F in full sun; Annie Oakley is a spineless, compact hybrid okra that produces early (52 days) and prolifically on plants 3–4 feet tall — smaller than most standard okra and suitable for smaller gardens or containers. The smooth, straight, dark-green pods are 5-ridged and should be harvested at 3–4 inches for maximum tenderness; pods become fibrous and woody if left beyond 5–6 inches. The spineless habit makes harvest comfortable without gloves; hand-pick every 2–3 days in summer heat. An AAS Award winner, Annie Oakley has proven reliable across the South and performs especially well in the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast summers.
sources: All-America Selections — Annie Oakley Okra Award Record
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Okra Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Okra Varieties

planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant after nights remain above 55°F. In zone 9 transplant February–March for a productive spring through fall season; New Mexico type chiles produce heavily through summer heat and can be roasted and frozen in quantity for year-round use.

directions: Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; the New Mexico type chile, particularly Hatch and its relatives, is the defining pepper of Southwestern cuisine, eaten green (roasted), red (dried), or ground into chile powder. This long, mildly hot pepper (1,500–3,000 SHU) averages 6–8 inches and is the standard for roasting — the thick walls blister perfectly under a broiler or over an open flame, and the roasted, peeled chiles freeze beautifully for months. Harvest green through September, or leave some to ripen red for a fully different flavor profile. The Hatch designation technically refers to chiles grown in the Hatch Valley of New Mexico, but the variety performs excellently throughout zone 9.
sources: New Mexico State University Extension — New Mexico Chile Varieties
Chile Pepper Institute — New Mexico Type Chile Varieties
University of Arizona Cooperative Extension — Chile Pepper Production

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; in zone 9 plant February–March for spring and August–September for fall. Beit Alpha is heat-tolerant and parthenocarpic (sets fruit without pollination), making it excellent for hot, enclosed or pest-challenged gardens.

directions: Trellis for best results; sow 3 seeds per hill 1 inch deep, thinning to the strongest when 3 inches tall. Beit Alpha is the classic Middle Eastern cucumber type — short (4–6 inches), thin-skinned, virtually seedless, and extremely crisp and sweet, with no bitterness even at room temperature. It is parthenocarpic, meaning it sets fruit without pollinator visits, and gynoecious (predominantly female flowers), resulting in extremely heavy production from compact vines. The thin skin requires no peeling and wilts less in high summer heat than standard American slicing cucumbers. Harvest frequently — the fruits reach ideal eating size within 24–48 hours in zone 9's summer heat and become puffy if left too long.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Specialty Cucumbers
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Beit Alpha
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Middle Eastern Cucumber Guide

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; in zone 9 plant March–May for spring and July–August for fall. Mississippi Crowder is a vigorous bush variety with higher tolerance for clay soils than many southern pea types — particularly valued in heavy Gulf Coast soils.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in any reasonably well-drained soil; Mississippi Crowder is a bush-type crowder pea with tightly packed seeds that turn light brown at maturity. It is a traditional Mississippi variety well-adapted to the heavy clay soils common along the Gulf Coast and Mississippi Delta, where it tolerates temporary waterlogging better than many other cowpea types. The crowder seeds develop a creamy texture when cooked with excellent flavor for fresh-shell eating, and they also dry well for storage as dried beans. The vigorous vines shade out weeds effectively once established, and the roots fix substantial nitrogen, improving soil for the following season.
sources: Mississippi State University Extension — Southern Pea Production
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Southern Pea Varieties
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Crowder Pea Varieties
